content.claim: Finland's comprehensive school reform (1972-1977) increased the tracking-age delay from 11 to 16 and was bundled with ability-grouping abolition (1985) and systemic curriculum/teacher-training changes; long-run evaluations show positive but modest effects on attainment and earnings, with only small improvements in test scores.
content.context: Fresh live research through the International comparison lens
limitations: Finland's reform was not a standalone tracking-age change but bundled with multiple structural reforms, making it difficult to isolate the causal effect of age delay alone.
Finland's homogeneous, high-trust institutional context and small population may not be replicable in Hungary's larger, more ethnically diverse, nationally uniform system.
Small positive test-score gains suggest that tracking-age delay alone (without accompanying curriculum/teacher reforms) may not guarantee large achievement improvements.
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
